name: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq
epithet: The One Who Set His Own Standard
title: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA): The One Who Set His Own Standard
meta_description: The story of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA), the truthful one, who believed without hesitation and quietly outdid everyone in good. How he set a standard for himself that no one else could measure.
keywords: Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, story of Abu Bakr, the truthful one, first caliph, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories, the firsts
lede: A delegation came to him with strange news, certain it would shake him. Your companion is claiming that in a single night he was carried from Makkah to Jerusalem and back. They waited for the doubt to land.

This is the story of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him): the truthful one, the man who believed without hesitating, and who spent his life quietly setting a standard for himself that no one around him could reach.

paras: His name among the people became as-Siddiq, the truthful one. It was not a title he chose. It was given to him because his speech and his actions never came apart. What he said matched what was real, and what he professed matched how he lived.

The Quran speaks of believers who are true to the promise they make to their Lord. Some are tested in that promise at once, some later, but their resolve never changes. That steadiness is the heart of what a siddiq is: a person who recognises the truth, commits to it, and then never wavers. There is a link, the scholars say, between a clean heart and a clear mind. When the heart is pure, the ability to see the truth becomes pure too, and in Abu Bakr the heart and the mind were entirely in step.

Many of us know the other half of that. We feel a surge of faith in Ramadan, on Hajj, in a moment of clarity, and we make promises to Allah. Then the feeling fades, and so does the promise. Abu Bakr was the man whose commitment did not fade when the feeling did.

sections.h2: "If he said it, he is telling the truth"
paras: The night the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was taken from Makkah to Jerusalem and shown the heavens, he worried on the way back that his people would not believe him. The angel Jibril reassured him: Abu Bakr will believe you, and he is the truthful one.

It happened exactly so. The Prophet ﷺ told the people, and they laughed and pointed and rushed to spread the news. Then they went to Abu Bakr, hoping to crack the small, persecuted community by making him stumble. Did your companion really say this, they asked, that he travelled to Jerusalem and back in one night.

He answered without pausing. "If he said that, then he is telling the truth." And he went further: "I believe him in something far greater than this. I believe him when he tells me revelation comes to him from the heavens. So why would this be hard for me to believe?" From that day the Prophet ﷺ called him as-Siddiq.

While the Muslims were still few and harassed in Makkah, a verse came down about a distant war. The Romans had just been crushed by the Persians, and it looked like a defeat they would never recover from. The verse promised the opposite: they would be defeated, then they would win.

To the people of Makkah this seemed an even harder claim than the night journey, because anyone could see the Romans were finished. Abu Bakr did not flinch. He went to the doubters and told them the Romans would return and win. One of them offered him a wager. Abu Bakr accepted, then raised the stakes to a hundred camels, a fortune he did not even fully own.

Years passed. The Romans looked more defeated than ever. Abu Bakr only grew more certain. When the believers migrated, he made sure the bet would still stand. In the end the Romans won, in the same season the Muslims won at Badr, and the wealth came to him. He gave every camel of it away in charity, and kept nothing for himself.

ayah.text: The Byzantines have been defeated in a nearby land. They will reverse their defeat with a victory in a few years' time. God is in command, first and last. On that day, the believers will rejoice at God's help. He helps whoever He pleases: He is the Mighty, the Merciful.

sections.h2: The one who saw what the Prophet ﷺ saw
paras: Abu Bakr's clarity was not only about belief. It was about vision. He was the first person the Prophet ﷺ would consult, and time after time his read of a situation matched the Prophet's own, even when no one else could see it.

At Hudaybiyyah, the Prophet ﷺ signed a peace treaty on terms that felt like humiliation, and the companions were stung. Abu Bakr was the one who saw what the Prophet ﷺ saw: that Islam would grow in peace, that an open road mattered more than wounded pride. He held that view when others could not.

His clarity held even at the hardest moment of his life. When the Prophet ﷺ died, no one was more broken than Abu Bakr. The community was reeling, and some could not accept that he was gone. Yet Abu Bakr was the one who steadied them, who reminded them that the message was meant to outlive the messenger. "Whoever used to worship Muhammad, Muhammad has died. Whoever used to worship Allah, Allah is ever living and never dies." He loved the Prophet ﷺ as much as anyone alive, and even in his grief he served the mission first. That was the gift of a heart so attached to the truth that it could not be knocked loose, even by the loss of the one he loved most.

sections.h2: The man who entered every gate
paras: One day the Prophet ﷺ described the gates of Paradise: a gate for those who excelled in prayer, a gate for charity, a gate for fasting, a gate for struggle. Whoever leads in one of these, he said, will be called from that gate. Abu Bakr asked, almost hoping, whether anyone could be called from all of them. The Prophet ﷺ answered: yes, and I hope you will be one of them.

It was not a wish without reason. On an ordinary morning the Prophet ﷺ asked his companions: who is fasting today, who followed a funeral, who fed a poor person, who visited someone sick. Each time, one man quietly answered that he had. It was Abu Bakr. The Prophet ﷺ said that no one combines all of these in a single day except that they enter Paradise.

He was not the strongest of them in body. He was slight, with a slender frame. Yet on the day of Badr he stood guard at the side of the Prophet ﷺ like a lion, letting no one near him. He led in every category that was open to everyone, and held virtues that belonged to him alone.

sections.h2: The house no one knew he visited
paras: Before he led the Muslims, Abu Bakr used to go and milk the goats of the widows and orphans of his neighbourhood. When he became the leader of the whole community, some of those women quietly worried that he would stop coming. He did not miss a single day. When people told him this was beneath him now, he refused the idea outright. "I would hate," he said, "that becoming leader would change a good habit in me, so that it could be said: he used to do this until he was put in charge, and then he left it."

Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) once admitted that he spent his nights trying to outdo Abu Bakr, and never managed it. When charity was called for, Umar brought half his wealth and felt sure he had finally won. Then Abu Bakr arrived with everything he owned. Asked what he had left for his family, he said: "Allah and His Messenger." Umar gave up the contest, saying he could never beat this man in anything.

After Abu Bakr became leader, Umar noticed him slip away every morning after the dawn prayer, walking out past the edge of the city instead of going home. The curiosity wore at him until one day he followed, keeping his distance. He watched Abu Bakr enter a worn-out little house and stay a long time, until the sun was high and the heat had set in, before walking back. Inside lived an old woman, blind and frail, with orphan children around her.

Umar asked who the man was who came each day. She did not know his name: he had never once told her. "May Allah reward him," she said. "Every morning he cleans my home, washes our clothes, grinds the wheat, bakes the bread, and cooks our breakfast, and then he leaves." Umar wept and said, "You have exhausted every leader who comes after you, Abu Bakr." We know of this house only because Umar happened to follow him. It is worth wondering how many other houses there were that no one ever followed him to.

It is tempting to explain Abu Bakr by counting his deeds: the prayers, the fasts, the fortune given away. But one of the early teachers said something closer to the truth. Abu Bakr did not outstrip everyone by how much he prayed and fasted. He outstripped them because of something in his heart.

That something was certainty in Allah's promise, and a quiet, constant longing to do better. When he saw an opening for good, he was first to recognise it and first to give himself to it completely. He did not wait for anyone else to clear the path. He set the standard, and then others followed.

That is why, all these centuries later, his life still presses on us. Sometimes the question is not how many extra prayers we managed or how much we put in the collection. Sometimes it is whether, in one honest moment, we saw a good and ran to it for the sake of Allah alone, asking for no one's notice.

lessons_heading: What Abu Bakr's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His greatness was not loud. It lived in a settled heart and a refusal to lower his own standard, and that is what makes it something an ordinary person can carry.

lessons.bold: Let your actions match your words.
lessons.text: He was called the truthful one because there was no gap between what he said and how he lived. Honesty is a whole way of being, not just a clean tongue.

lessons.bold: Set your own standard, and hold it.
lessons.text: He did not wait for others to lead. He saw the good first and committed to it fully, and his example pulled everyone around him upward.

lessons.bold: Keep good that only Allah can see.
lessons.text: He led the prayer in public and, before dawn, served a blind woman who never learned his name. Hidden good keeps the heart honest.

lessons.bold: Do not let a new role end an old good habit.
lessons.text: Becoming the most important man in the community did not stop him milking the goats of widows. He refused to let status retire his kindness.

faq.a: He was the closest companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the first adult man to accept Islam, and the first leader of the Muslims after the Prophet's death. He is known as as-Siddiq, the truthful one.

faq.q: Why is Abu Bakr called as-Siddiq?
faq.a: When the Prophet ﷺ told the people of the night journey to Jerusalem and back, others mocked it. Abu Bakr accepted it at once, saying that if the Prophet ﷺ said it, it was true. From then he was known as the truthful one.

faq.q: What was the bet about the Romans?
faq.a: A verse foretold that the defeated Romans would soon win again. Abu Bakr was so certain of it that he accepted a wager of a hundred camels with a doubter. When the Romans won, he gave the entire amount away in charity.

faq.q: What can we learn from Abu Bakr's life?
faq.a: To keep our actions true to our words, to set a high standard for ourselves rather than wait for others, to guard private good that only Allah sees, and to never let a new position end a good habit.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Abu Bakr was promised Paradise, yet he never coasted. He kept finding the next good and reaching for it, not because anyone was watching, but because his heart could not rest while there was good left undone. That is the part that is hard to forget.

Most of our lives are made of ordinary mornings. His were too, and he filled them with small, unseen acts of care and a standard he set for no one but himself. The invitation in his story is simple: when you notice a good today, do it quietly, do it for Allah, and do not wait for anyone else to go first.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), part two. The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 30:2-5). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
A delegation came to him, certain they could shake him.

"Your companion says he was carried to Jerusalem and back in one night."

They waited for the doubt.

He did not pause.

"If he said that, then he is telling the truth."

From that day the Prophet ﷺ called him **as-Siddiq**: the truthful one.

A verse promised the defeated Romans would win again.

Everyone could see they were finished. Abu Bakr bet a hundred camels that they would return.

They did. He gave every camel to charity.

At the hardest moment, when the Prophet ﷺ died and the community was breaking, he was the one who stood and steadied them.

No one loved him more. No one served the mission faster.

One ordinary day the Prophet ﷺ asked: who fasted today, who followed a funeral, who fed the poor, who visited the sick?

Each time, one quiet voice answered. It was Abu Bakr.

As leader, he kept slipping out before sunrise.

Umar followed him once, to a worn little house. Inside: a blind woman and orphans.

He cleaned, washed, baked, and left. She never knew his name.

"You have exhausted every leader who comes after you, Abu Bakr."

Umar wept when he understood what the man did every single morning.

**Set your own standard, and hold it.**

He saw the good first and ran to it, for Allah alone, asking no one's notice.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
When the people of Makkah tried to break the early Muslims, they went to one man, sure he would crack. They told Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) that the Prophet ﷺ claimed he had travelled to Jerusalem and back in a single night. His answer was instant: "If he said it, then he is telling the truth."

That was the whole of him. He believed without hesitating, he gave away fortunes without keeping count, and as leader of the community he still rose before dawn to quietly serve a blind woman who never learned his name. He set a standard for himself that no one around him could reach.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: "If he said it, then he is telling the truth"
email.alt_subject: The man who set his own standard
email.preview: They came to break him with strange news. He did not pause for a second.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

When the people of Makkah wanted to shake the small, persecuted community of Muslims, they went to one man, certain he would stumble.

They told Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him) that the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was claiming he had been carried from Makkah to Jerusalem and back in a single night. They waited for the doubt to land.

He did not pause. "If he said that, then he is telling the truth." And he went further: "I believe him in something far greater than this. I believe him when he says revelation comes to him from the heavens." From that day, the Prophet ﷺ called him as-Siddiq, the truthful one.

That was the whole of him. When a verse promised that the defeated Romans would win again, Abu Bakr was so certain he wagered a fortune on it, and when it came true, he gave every camel of it away. When the Prophet ﷺ died and the community was breaking, he was the one who stood and steadied everyone, though no one was more heartbroken than him.

But the part that stays with me is quieter. Even after he became leader of the whole community, he kept slipping out before sunrise. Umar followed him one morning, all the way to a worn little house at the edge of the city. Inside lived a blind woman with orphan children. Abu Bakr cleaned her home, washed the clothes, ground the wheat, baked the bread, and cooked breakfast, and then walked back to lead the people. She never knew his name.

When people told him such things were beneath his new position, he refused. "I would hate," he said, "that becoming leader would change a good habit in me."

One of the early teachers explained him best. Abu Bakr did not outstrip everyone by how much he prayed and fasted. He outstripped them because of something in his heart: certainty in Allah, and a longing to do better that never rested. He set his own standard, and held it.

May Allah be pleased with him, and grant us a little of that same longing for good.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: A delegation came to him, sure they could shake him. Your companion is claiming, they said, that in one night he was carried from Makkah to Jerusalem and back. They waited for the doubt to land.

Abu Bakr as-Siddiq (may Allah be pleased with him) did not pause. "If he said that," he answered, "then he is telling the truth." And he added that he already believed the Prophet ﷺ in something far greater: that revelation came to him from the heavens. From that day he was known as the truthful one.

His certainty showed again and again. When a verse foretold that the crushed Romans would rise and win, he was so sure that he wagered a hundred camels on it, a fortune he did not even fully own. When the prediction came true, he gave every camel away in charity and kept nothing.

He was the one whose vision matched the Prophet's own. At Hudaybiyyah, when a hard peace stung the others, Abu Bakr saw what the Prophet ﷺ saw. And when the Prophet ﷺ died and the community began to break, it was Abu Bakr, heartbroken as he was, who stood and steadied everyone.

He led in every kind of good. On one ordinary day the Prophet ﷺ asked who had fasted, who had followed a funeral, who had fed the poor, who had visited the sick. Each time, one quiet voice answered that he had. It was Abu Bakr.

Even as leader, he kept slipping out before dawn. Umar followed him once, to a worn little house where a blind woman lived with orphan children. Abu Bakr cleaned the home, washed the clothes, baked the bread, and left, and she never learned his name. "You have exhausted every leader who comes after you," Umar wept.

One of the early teachers said it simply. Abu Bakr did not outstrip everyone by how much he prayed and fasted, but by something in his heart: certainty in Allah, and a longing to do better that never rested.
storybook.pullquote: If he said that, then he is telling the truth.
storybook.reflection: Abu Bakr was promised Paradise, yet he never stopped reaching for the next good, often where no one could see him. His life asks us a gentle question: when we notice a good today, will we run to it quietly, for Allah alone, without waiting for anyone else to go first?
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts, part two.
